speaker: interviewer
text: Today is October 7, 1996. The survivor is Judith Velencei, maiden name, Schvarcz. Interviewer, Phyllis Stoleroff, in the city of Dover, in the state of New Jersey, and the country of the United States of America. The language spoken is English. My name is Phyllis Stoleroff-- S-T-O-L-E-R-O-F-F. I have the privilege of interviewing Judith Velencei, maiden name, Schvarcz, on October 7, 1996, Dover, New Jersey, in the United States. The interview is in English. Hello. Can you please tell us your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Judith Velencei.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you please spell it?

speaker: interviewee
text: V-E-L-E-N-C-E-I.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your full name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Judith Schvarcz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Spell it, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: S-C-H-V-A-R-C-Z.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you ever known by any other name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Judith Sarvari. S-A-R-V-A-R-I.

speaker: interviewer
text: When were you known by that name?

speaker: interviewee
text: After the war, my parents decided to change the Schvarcz to a Hungarian name.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what year were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1932, February 26.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what city?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a little village. Tibolddaroc.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: T-I-L-B-O-R-- no. D. B-O-R-D-A-R-C.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your-- your mother's maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Klima. Her first name is Jozefa.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she have a middle name?

speaker: interviewee
text: She had many. And the next one to Jozefa was Maria.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you spell Klima?

speaker: interviewee
text: Klima-- K-L-I-M-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: At birth, Ignac Schvarcz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when he changed his name, it became?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ignac Istvan Sarvari.

speaker: interviewer
text: His occupation before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Main occupation was to do with wine. We had winery and he was an importer/exporter of wine. And also, we happened to own a power station, which he started in that little village, which had no electricity.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the name of the village again?

speaker: interviewee
text: Tibolddaroc.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. How many siblings were in your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only my brother and me.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. And your brother's name was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Otto.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he was known by what last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: At birth, Schvarcz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And later on?

speaker: interviewee
text: And later, together with us, Sarvari.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have another sister at any time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I did-- do have a sister, but she was born after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her name was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Tamara.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is her-- what is her name now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her-- she's Tamara Szabo. S-Z-A-B-O.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where is she living?

speaker: interviewee
text: She live in Debrecen, Hungary.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where does your brother live?

speaker: interviewee
text: She live in Szolnok, Hungary.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you ever married?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your husband's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: John Velencei.

speaker: interviewer
text: When were you married?

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Miskolc.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that please?

speaker: interviewee
text: M-I-S-K-O-L-C.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he a survivor?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. He was not Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he Hungarian?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, he was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Judith, your testimony is very important, so please tell us about your story as you recall it. First, where is the village you were born in located?

speaker: interviewee
text: It is between Miskolc and Eger, in a bottom of a big mountain. [NON-ENGLISH] Very remote. Very rural. It had slightly over 2,000 people. But they didn't had no train, no bus, even the main road leading into the town was a dirt road. So there is no more rural a town can get as Tibolddaroc was before the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: How far was it from Budapest?

speaker: interviewee
text: About 160 mile--

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: Sorry, kilometer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm. How far was it from any other major cities?

speaker: interviewee
text: Miskolc was about 30, 30-some kilometer. And Mezokovesd, the county seat, were about 20, 25 kilometers.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how far was it from the Czechoslovakian border?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it was quite far. I would say around 200 kilometer or more.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are your earliest recollections of family life in the town?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think my memory go back pretty good. And I had an extremely happy childhood. Matter of fact, I always said, I don't think I ever met anybody who had as beautiful and perfect family life and so much happiness as I had as a young child. We were raised very liberal, which some people didn't agree those days. But my-- our parents didn't put much restriction on us. And we were closely supervised and corrected if we did something wrong. But we were very free to roam around and do whatever we pleased. And my parents were highly respected citizens of the town. And we grew up with a kind of admiration and we felt that from that town people. And we were very free to go into anybody's house. We were welcomed everyplace. But our family life was closely structured by the Jewish religion. My grandparents were very strict Orthodox Jewish family. And my father was the first one who kind of stopped that orthodoxy and became more reformed. And he caused quite a bit of uproar when he married my mom, who was Catholic. But my mom decided to change her religion to become Jewish, just for the smoothness of the family life. It was so much easier. And she loved my father. And I'm sure it wasn't very hard on her to do it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So she was officially becoming Jewish. And she did her very best to keep kosher house. She learned Hebrew. Matter of fact, she was the lead prayer in the little house of prayer, because no other Jewish lady in town was so good in Hebrew as she was. She learned a lot from our private tutor. My father and some others in the town hired a Hebrew teacher, who was living with us, and who constantly coached us in prayers and in Hebrew. And we observed all the Jewish holidays, which I think is favoring the children. Because on Saturdays, nobody allowed to work. So what else is left? To playing with the children. And we were surrounded with grown-ups with organized games in a Saturday afternoon. And I remember that unity we had, which was really beautiful.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we did had a couple of nice kids of our age in that group. The doctor who lived across the street had two children exactly the same old as we were. The son was my age and the daughter was my brother's age. And we constantly played together. And also, we had many cousins, boys and girls. So we never ran out of playmates. And as you asked me about child life, I think I had just about the most perfect life anybody could ask for.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you think back about the community, was-- what was the ethnic breakdown in the town?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was dominated by Catholics. The town consisted of slightly over 2,000 people. And out of the 2,000, at least 1,600 or more were Catholic. And around 300 or more were Protestants. And the Jewish community was small. I remember around 16 families, which might be more, but that's how I-- as much as I can recall. But some of the family had large siblings, so I think it was around 60 or so people, Jewish, in that town. And it-- as I said, it was a poor town, very remote. And the Jewish community was the-- practically the middle class of the town. And they were shopkeepers, businessmens, one of them had a soda factory. They made, you know, that came in-- in a glass bottle so you could refill it. But this was just about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall what your father's wineries looked like at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. I spent many, many hours with him there. It was, from the outside, a very simple building, stone building. But inside, he had an office and a long, long wine basement, which went right into this mountain. How long it was, I really don't know. Because I was never allowed to go. But I am not-- I can estimate close to half a mile. He may not use that all the way, but it was a very, very deep cellar. He spend, certain time of the year, couple days in there, when he was testing the wine. But because of his occupation, he was mostly home. He only had to make a couple of telephone calls to get a contract. But he spent his day playing with us, supervising us, and teaching us. And I think that was just about as good as can be. Because I adored my father. And I was a little shadow for him. Not so much my brother, who was following my mom. And he even learned embroidery and stayed beside my mother. And they talked. She would read stories for him. He was exclusively hers and I was exclusively my father's. And we never had jealousy between us. So it was good agreement.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you recall about your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, she was a lovely lady. She was beautiful, and very elegant, and very dignified. She was spoiled. She didn't do much housework. She never worked outside the house. And she had time to entertain us.

speaker: interviewee
text: So every afternoon, even the neighborhood kids would come over. And she would read stories or make up stories, especially if she learned one of us were unruly. And she would make up stories to-- to teach us through the story and give us lessons. But it was-- it was just nice. Do you recall what the synagogue in town looked like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I still remember that. I guess they bought just an ordinary peasant house, a big peasant house, and the back part of it was extended. And it was much bigger as we needed in town. I guess they were planning to grew in size. Because I never remember seeing it full. The women's were upstairs. And it was a very primitive, narrow, wooden stairway going upstairs. And I did go with my mom numerous time. And I remember that heavy, green drapery which was put up between the women's section. It was small if you look at nationwide, but it was pretty nice. They had modern chairs and the woodwork inside was pretty nice. I remember the place they kept the Torah and it was very fancy for my childhood memory. It-- it looked really nice. I proud to go into that house of prayer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any contact with the Catholic community of the town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes. I kept a very close contact because of my catholic grandmother and because of their aunt. Matter of fact, we were really-- some people criticized us on the liberty, but, in many time, I accompanied them to the Catholic church. So Saturday, I would go to the Jewish prayers. And Sunday, I would go to the Catholics'. And I would just sit there and admire all of that ceremony which went on. And people looked at me with approval and nobody made no comment on it. Everybody in town knew I am Jewish. But it was just natural. I was also belonged to my other grandmother. And I remember Christmastime. We used to celebrate both. We used to celebrate Hanukkah and also we would have a Christmas tree. The only restriction we did had-- we put the Christmas tree into the very end room in the house. And we were taught by my parents-- don't brag about it. Please don't mention your cousins. Because they may feel jealous, you receiving present on Christmastime and they don't. And it would hurt them. So don't say it. And we never did. It was almost like a little family secret. But we celebrated practically all the holidays. I remember when the springtime-- I think it was on Passover-- when my mother changed dishes. And we had beautiful porcelain in attic. And she would bring it down. And everything became so festive. But in the same time, the Christmas was just as festive for us. So, it just show you, you can put the two together in peace. Every evening, we were taught to say the Hebrew evening prayer and also the Catholic-- my mother teached us how to say how God bless my parents. And we would include all the Jewish cousins and aunts and uncles into our Catholic prayer. So it-- it didn't cause no conflict. I never felt-- matter of fact, it-- I felt I belonged to both community. When I was with the Jewish group, I was so thoroughly Jewish. But when I went to my Catholic grandmother, I felt like I belonged to that group. I never felt conflict. I don't know how. But that's-- many people ask me previously about that. But it certainly didn't happen to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And all this time, what was the spoken language at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: We only spoke Hungarian. Matter of fact, I never even knew my father is fluent in Yiddish, till I was at least eight years old. And one day, my father answered the phone. And he started to spoke a language I never heard before.

speaker: interviewee
text: I could hardly wait for her-- for him to put down the phone. And I said, Dad, what did you talk? What kind of language was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said, it was Yiddish. He talked to someone who only spoke Yiddish. And that's when I realized he speak that too. Later, when the Russians came in, I find out he also spoke Russian. (LAUGHS)

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of schooling did you have at that time, since you were only between six and eight?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there was a government restriction. And you was obligated to go to the local-- to the closest school-- government school-- which was available. And that way, we belonged to that school which was in town. And that was a Catholic-sponsored government school. And for the first four years-- first four grade-- we both of us went to that school. But after you are 10, there were private schools available to children to go to. And because commuting was impossible from our town to any city which had school, both my brother and I, we went to boarding schools.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about the Catholic school. What was that like? What did you learn there?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Catholic school-- it was small. They only had three rooms. The first and second grade were together, the third and fourths, and the fifths and sixth. And that's as far as it went. And it had only three teachers. And we didn't took schooling very seriously. By the time I went to school, I was completely-- knew how to read and write. And the school didn't offer too much what we didn't learn at home, between the Hebrew teacher, and between my father constant tutoring. So school, the first four grade, was pretty boring for both my brother and I. But the teachers realized that. And we spend many days away from school. If my parents happened to go for the week to Budapest, we went with them. Or any time they had some kind of a-- a activity, we were free to stay home. They never held against us. If it was a Jewish holiday or something, we just stayed home. Or-- in-- anyway, they were very liberal toward us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So-- you might-- so the town-- the town's-- what was the town's total attitude towards the Jewish families at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very good. I think it had absolutely no anti-Semitism in those days. The people were very poor. They had more trouble to survive instead of worrying about somebody's other religion or other customs. They had no radio. Most houses had no electricity. So they didn't hear no propaganda. And the town was very primitive. And I never experienced, before the war, any anti-Semitism in there. But I think the Jewish community also took a pretty serious step so it wouldn't become-- nobody flaunted money, including my father. I find out much later how much money he had. And he could have build much bigger and more fancier house. And we could've lived a far more elegant life. And he chose not to. And if I heard any argument, ever, between my father and mother, it was always on that ground. My mother wanted to move out of the town. She wasn't born there. And she was anxious to go into the city. And my father never thought of it. Because my grandmother was alive. And till she was alive, he wasn't about to bring the family into any kind of move.

speaker: interviewer
text: In 1938, were you still in school in the town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes I was. Only in 1939, when the war broke out, when the first time we heard anything about anti-Semitism. And I have to say my father was extremely wise. And he made very early all kind of a preparation for our future. He was very careful to save as much money as possible. He wouldn't let us get any new thing. My mother-- new furniture or anything like that, not to-- not to arouse no bad feelings. And he was getting prepared for-- if anything may happen, he tried to be as ready as possible. He was drafted into the army soon after the war broke out.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year was that?

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you living at home then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Or were you at boarding school at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I was still at home. And my mother was very-- putting pressure on my father about my future and my brother's. She wanted us to be christened. She was very anxious to move out of town, into a big city, where it would be able to start a new life as Catholics. But my father would not hear of it. Eventually, when things got really bad-- I think it was already in '39-- when I was christened. And after that, my mother made preparation for my schooling and she send me into a boarding school, into Kosice, which was a Catholic, nun-run institution. So she was doing that on purpose, so I could establish my way in a more Catholic world.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the name of that town that you went to school--

speaker: interviewee
text: Kosice. And the name of the school was [NON-ENGLISH]. It was at Latin title. And it was run by Catholic nuns. It was a small school, very elegant, very expensive, those days. And-- but my mother fought for that. And she assumed that would be the right education for me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh-- can we stop right here now.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we'll continue on--

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: --on the next tape.
